01a_quotes.title: Creativity in the Age of AI: Evaluating the Impact of Generative AI on Design Outputs and Designers' Creative Thinking

study_design: We conducted a within-subjects experiment where we asked participants to design advertisements both with and without GenAI support.
We conducted a within-subjects experimental study, in which 36 participants designed social media advertisements with and without support from GenAI.
All participants participated in both sessions, with session order counterbalanced.

The experiment consisted of two sessions. In one session, participants designed the advertisement with the support of GenAI (GenAI-supported condition, text-to-image model DALL-E 2 using the ChatGPT web app), while in the other, they designed without GenAI support (human-only condition).
All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced to control for any order effects.
The study consisted of a two-phase, within-subjects experiment, which lasted approximately 90 minutes.
task: Participants were asked to complete a common real-world design task: creating advertising material for a social media campaign to promote a client organization.
Participants were tasked with creating a 1080 × 1080 pixel advertisement for a social media advertising campaign to promote a newly launched research group website.
In each case, participants had 25 minutes to produce one to two designs.
Each participant submitted one or two designs per session.
population: We recruited 36 participants via professional and academic communication channels (Slack, Discord, etc.), email lists, and fliers posted on a large public university campus in the United States.
Participants were over-representative of young people, with 94% (# = 34) under the age of 30, and of women (81%, # = 29).
They had a diverse range of AI design tool experience with 67% (# = 24) having at least some experience using GenAI for design tasks.
Each participant received a $40 Amazon gift card for completing the study.
arms: In one session, participants designed the advertisement with the support of GenAI (GenAI-supported condition, text-to-image model DALL-E 2 using the ChatGPT web app), while in the other, they designed without GenAI support (human-only condition).
Phase 1: A randomly selected half of all participants were first assigned to design individually, with the second half assigned to design with GenAI.
The GenAI group was instructed to use ChatGPT for design support but was also permitted to use other tools to correct inaccuracies in text generated by AI or to clip images as needed.
The human-only group worked without AI assistance.

Phase 2. This phase mirrored the first, with participants completing the condition they did not complete initially: those who used ChatGPT initially (GenAI-supported condition) now designed individually (human-only condition) and vice versa.
outcome_definitions: After each session, we measured their mood (assessed through self-reported energy, relaxation, and pleasure), inherent creative ability (with one measure for divergent thinking and another for convergent thinking, as detailed in Section 3.2), and self-reported self-efficacy.

The experts rated each design for its visual appeal, alignment with the client's brand, creativity, unconventionality (operationalized as "weirdness"), and perceived usefulness for a social media campaign.

To measure participants' inherent creativity, we used the Alternative Uses Test (AUT), an established measure of divergent thinking, and the Anagram Test, an established measure of convergent thinking.
In the AUT, a participant brainstorms unconventional uses for a commonplace item, such as a bottle or table, with a two-minute time limit.
The total number of uses the participant lists across both objects is their final score.
To assess convergent thinking, we used the Anagram Test. In this task, the participant rearranges the letters of a prompt word (e.g., "lamp") into a different word (e.g., "palm").
They have three minutes to rearrange as many prompt words as possible into their anagrams; the number of completed words is their final score.
We measured participant mood via a short, self-report survey with three subscales: relaxation, pleasure, and energy level.
We also measured participant self-efficacy through a self-report measure developed by Dow et al.
To evaluate the quality of a design by experts, we created a five-question survey to be completed by five expert reviewers blind to experiment condition.

This included an 11-point scale question about the usefulness of the design ("In a social media ad campaign, how well will this ad perform?"), and four 7-point "strongly degree" to "strong agree questions" to measure designs' creativity ("This design is creative"), weirdness ("This design is weird '"), representing of the client ("This design effectively represents [anonymous organization for which it was designed], ") and visual appeal ("This design is visually appealing ").
statistical_results: In total, participants generated 110 designs (54 designs in the human-only condition and 56 designs in the GenAI-supported condition).
Five designs were filtered out for non-compliance with the study requirements.
This resulted in a final count of 105 eligible designs—52 human-only and 53 GenAI-supported—for design evaluation.
The average number of designs per participant was 1.44 in the human-only condition and 1.47 in the GenAI-supported condition.

GenAI-supported designs were perceived by experts as significantly more creative and unconventional than human-only designs, though we observed no difference between conditions in usefulness (U = 0.238, p = .812), visual appeal (U = 0.456, p = .649), or alignment with the client's organization (U = 1.213, p = .225).

Average creativity ratings for GenAI-supported designs (M = 3.909, SD = 1.389) were rated 13% more creative than the designs from the human-only condition (M = 3.457, SD = 1.359, U = 3.318, p < .001).

Average unconventionality (operationalized as "weirdness") of GenAI-supported designs (M = 4.262, SD = 1.328) was 23.3% more unconventional than the designs from human-only condition (M = 3.457, SD = 1.250, U = 5.958, p < .001).
For participants without prior GenAI design tool experience, using GenAI for a 25-minute design session increased their divergent thinking ability.

Our analysis showed a significant increase in idea fluency score (M = 14.333, SD = 4.384) and flexibility score (M = 12.250, SD = 3.562) after the GenAI-supported design session, as compared to the human-only design session (fluency, M = 12.500, SD = 4.573, U = 2.112, p < .05; flexibility, M = 10.167, SD = 3.131, U = 2.203, p < .05).
However, for participants with prior GenAI design tool experience, there was no significant difference in divergent thinking between conditions.
For native English speaker participants, our analysis showed that using GenAI for a 25-minute design session decreased their feeling of relaxation.

Our analysis found that the mean relaxation score for native English speakers after the designing with GenAI session was 3.375 (SD = 1.467), compared with the mean relaxation score of 4.250 (SD = 1.199) after the human-only design session.

In addition, for native English speakers, there was a marginally significant difference in pleasure between conditions, with pleasure being higher in the human-only condition (M = 4.833, SD = 1.404) than in the GenAI-supported condition (M = 4.208, SD = 1.290, U = 1.741, p = 0.082).
However, for participants who were non-native speakers, there were no significant differences between the two experimental conditions.
Condition did not affect other measurements, such as divergent and convergent thinking, self-efficacy, and energy level for either non-native or native speakers.

experts evaluated GenAI-supported designs as more creative and unconventional ("weird") than human-only designs, but designs did not significantly differ between conditions in their perceived usefulness, visual appeal, or alignment with the client's organization.
Our primary analyses showed no significant impact of GenAI on participants' inherent creative abilities, mood, or self-efficacy.
a post-study independent samples Wilcoxon signed-rank test revealed that self-reported design skills did not significantly affect the perceived usefulness of designs (U = 0.238, p = 0.812)
ambiguity_or_caveats: One limitation of our study was the reliance on expert evaluations to assess the quality of design products.
While heuristic expert evaluations can serve as a proxy of usefulness, they may not fully align with real-world performance metrics, such as click-through rates on social media platforms.

Another constraint was our use of the GPT-4 model powered by DALL-E2 for text-to-image generation, rather than employing CSTs powered by multimodal language models specifically designed to enhance creative thinking.
While ChatGPT was chosen for its prevalence and accessibility, recent research has shown that the text-to-image model possesses biases.
Future research should investigate the variable impacts of text-to-image GenAI models on creativity and whether design outputs are influenced by different models or not.
We initially planned to analyze the influence of GenAI on participants with varying self-reported design skills.

However, we abandoned this plan because: 1) our recruiting did not yield a participant pool with sufficiently diverse design expertise; 2) the survey question used to assess self-reported design skills lacked a clear benchmark for comparison; 3) a post-study independent samples Wilcoxon signed-rank test revealed that self-reported design skills did not significantly affect the perceived usefulness of designs (U = 0.238, p = 0.812), or in other words, high self-reported designers do not produce better design outcomes.

Consequently, we shifted our approach to examine GenAI's impact across the entire participant group and within subgroups that are more accurately defined, such as native language and prior GenAI design tool exposure.

[Authors' note: we plan to present design examples in the paper to illustrate the quantitative and qualitative results, but the advertisements contain the client's name which can be associated with the authors' institution. To maintain anonymity, we will not show these design examples in the current manuscript. We will coordinate with the ACs to understand if and how we can show these examples during the later review phase.]

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical within-subjects lab experiment with 36 designers. The study measures both design output quality (via expert evaluation) and designer cognitive/affective outcomes (via standardized tests and self-report). Key finding: GenAI-supported designs rated as more creative and unconventional but not more useful; effects on designer creativity vary by prior AI experience and native language. The paper includes subgroup analyses revealing differential impacts across user populations.

01_extraction.title: Creativity in the Age of AI: Evaluating the Impact of Generative AI on Design Outputs and Designers' Creative Thinking
01_extraction.study_design_description: A within-subjects experimental study in which 36 participants designed social media advertisements both with and without GenAI support. The experiment consisted of two sessions lasting approximately 90 minutes total. All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced to control for order effects. In one session, participants designed with support from GenAI (text-to-image model DALL-E 2 using the ChatGPT web app); in the other, they designed without GenAI support.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Participants were tasked with creating a 1080 × 1080 pixel advertisement for a social media advertising campaign to promote a newly launched research group website. In each session, participants had 25 minutes to produce one to two designs.
01_extraction.population_description: 36 participants recruited via professional and academic communication channels (Slack, Discord, etc.), email lists, and fliers posted on a large public university campus in the United States. Participants were over-representative of young people (94% under age 30) and women (81%). They had diverse AI design tool experience, with 67% having at least some experience using GenAI for design tasks.

arms.description: Participants designed the advertisement with the support of GenAI (text-to-image model DALL-E 2 using the ChatGPT web app). The GenAI group was instructed to use ChatGPT for design support but was also permitted to use other tools to correct inaccuracies in text generated by AI or to clip images as needed.
Participants designed without GenAI support. The human-only group worked without AI assistance.

outcomes.outcome_description: Expert rating of design creativity on a 7-point scale ("strongly disagree" to "strongly agree") to the statement "This design is creative".
Expert rating of design unconventionality (operationalized as "weirdness") on a 7-point scale ("strongly disagree" to "strongly agree") to the statement "This design is weird".
Expert rating of design usefulness on an 11-point scale in response to the question "In a social media ad campaign, how well will this ad perform?"
Expert rating of design visual appeal on a 7-point scale ("strongly disagree" to "strongly agree") to the statement "This design is visually appealing".

Expert rating of alignment with the client's organization on a 7-point scale ("strongly disagree" to "strongly agree") to the statement "This design effectively represents [anonymous organization for which it was designed]".

Divergent thinking ability measured via the Alternative Uses Test (AUT), where participants brainstorm unconventional uses for commonplace items with a two-minute time limit. The total number of uses listed across both objects is the final score.
Divergent thinking flexibility measured via the Alternative Uses Test (AUT), assessing the variety of categories of uses generated.

Convergent thinking ability measured via the Anagram Test, where participants rearrange letters of prompt words into different words with a three-minute time limit. The number of completed words is the final score.
Participant mood assessed through self-reported relaxation via a short self-report survey.
Participant mood assessed through self-reported pleasure via a short self-report survey.
Participant mood assessed through self-reported energy level via a short self-report survey.
Participant self-efficacy measured through a self-report measure developed by Dow et al.

Average unconventionality (operationalized as "weirdness") of GenAI-supported designs (M = 4.262, SD = 1.328) was 23.3% more unconventional than the designs from human-only condition (M = 3.457, SD = 1.250, U = 5.958, p < .001).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: genai_supported vs human_only (participants without prior GenAI design tool experience)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: genai_supported vs human_only (participants without prior GenAI design tool experience)
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Our analysis showed a significant increase in idea fluency score (M = 14.333, SD = 4.384) and flexibility score (M = 12.250, SD = 3.562) after the GenAI-supported design session, as compared to the human-only design session (fluency, M = 12.500, SD = 4.573, U = 2.112, p < .05; flexibility, M = 10.167, SD = 3.131, U = 2.203, p < .05).

Our analysis found that the mean relaxation score for native English speakers after the designing with GenAI session was 3.375 (SD = 1.467), compared with the mean relaxation score of 4.250 (SD = 1.199) after the human-only design session. The difference was significant (U = 2.481, p = .013).

In addition, for native English speakers, there was a marginally significant difference in pleasure between conditions, with pleasure being higher in the human-only condition (M = 4.833, SD = 1.404) than in the GenAI-supported condition (M = 4.208, SD = 1.290, U = 1.741, p = 0.082).

01_extraction.notes: This is a within-subjects design with 36 participants; each participant completed both conditions (GenAI-supported and human-only), so arm-level n is not separately reported in the quotes. The study generated 110 total designs (54 human-only, 56 GenAI-supported), with 5 filtered for non-compliance, leaving 105 eligible designs (52 human-only, 53 GenAI-supported) for expert evaluation. Several outcomes showed no significant differences: design usefulness, visual appeal, brand alignment, and (for participants with prior GenAI experience) divergent thinking. Subgroup analyses revealed differential effects: divergent thinking improvements were significant only for participants without prior GenAI design tool experience; mood effects (relaxation and pleasure) were significant only for native English speakers. The paper notes that self-reported design skills did not significantly affect perceived usefulness of designs (U = 0.238, p = 0.812) and were therefore not used as a subgroup variable in final analyses.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly states it is "A within-subjects experimental study" where "All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced." Each participant designed both with and without GenAI support, making this a clear within-subject design.

per_outcome.operationalization: Expert rating on 7-point scale (strongly disagree to strongly agree) to statement "This design is creative"
Expert rating on 7-point scale (strongly disagree to strongly agree) to statement "This design is weird"
Expert rating on 11-point scale responding to "In a social media ad campaign, how well will this ad perform?"
Expert rating on 7-point scale (strongly disagree to strongly agree) to statement "This design is visually appealing"
Expert rating on 7-point scale (strongly disagree to strongly agree) to statement about effective representation of the organization
Total number of unconventional uses listed for commonplace items in Alternative Uses Test with two-minute time limit
Variety of categories of uses generated in Alternative Uses Test, scored as flexibility measure
Number of completed anagrams in three-minute Anagram Test where participants rearrange letters into different words
Self-reported relaxation level via short survey
Self-reported pleasure level via short survey
Self-reported energy level via short survey
Self-report measure developed by Dow et al., not further specified

04_onet.rationale: The participants were 'tasked with creating a 1080 × 1080 pixel advertisement for a social media advertising campaign' — this is fundamentally a creative design task requiring original visual composition and conceptual development. While the output is digital and involves design tools, the core work activity being measured is creative ideation and execution, not technical software operation or graphic design as an occupation. The 25-minute timeframe and requirement to produce 'one to two designs' emphasizes the creative generation aspect rather than the full scope of a professional graphic designer's role.

alternates.onet_label: Drafting, Laying Out, and Specifying Technical Devices, Parts, and Equipment

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm available: genai_supported, which uses DALL-E 2 via ChatGPT for design support.
llm_extracted.notes: SALVAGE PASS: Found sample size n=36 participants in the Methods section (page 6): 'We recruited 36 participants via professional and academic communication channels'. This is a within-subjects design where all participants completed both conditions (human-only and GenAI-supported) in counterbalanced order. The paper states 'All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced' (page 6). The test statistic reported is Mann-Whitney U = 3.318, which appears to be an error since Mann-Whitney is for independent samples, not paired data. However, means and SDs are provided for both conditions. For within-subjects designs with n=36 paired observations, we can compute Hedges' g using the paired-samples formula. The p-value is reported as inequality 'p < .001' rather than exact value. Direction convention is larger_is_better since creativity is rated on a 7-point scale where higher values indicate more creativity.
source_quotes: We recruited 36 participants via professional and academic communication channels (Slack, Discord, etc.), email lists, and fliers posted on a large public university campus in the United States.
All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced.

Average creativity ratings for GenAI-supported designs (M = 3.909, SD = 1.389) were rated 13% more creative than the designs from the human-only condition (M = 3.457, SD = 1.359, U = 3.318, p < .001).

computed.notes: SALVAGE PASS: Found sample size n=36 participants in the Methods section (page 6): 'We recruited 36 participants via professional and academic communication channels'. This is a within-subjects design where all participants completed both conditions (human-only and GenAI-supported) in counterbalanced order. The paper states 'All participants completed both sessions, with the order of conditions counterbalanced' (page 6). The test statistic reported is Mann-Whitney U = 3.318, which appears to be an error since Mann-Whitney is for independent samples, not paired data. However, means and SDs are provided for both conditions. For within-subjects designs with n=36 paired observations, we can compute Hedges' g using the paired-samples formula. The p-value is reported as inequality 'p < .001' rather than exact value. Direction convention is larger_is_better since creativity is rated on a 7-point scale where higher values indicate more creativity.
